channel.title: Ilia Klishin Publications Feed
channel.description: Publication cards with concise summaries and links to original sources.

item.title: Putin-Macron talks and the Russia expertise gap
item.description:
In this Feb 14, 2022 Moscow Times opinion piece, Ilya Klishin argues that a small factual error in foreign reporting around Putin-Macron talks exposed a larger expertise problem in commentary about Russia. The trigger is a repeated claim that Putin quoted a specific punk song after his meeting with Emmanuel Macron.
It connects media analysis with the original text published by The Moscow Times.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2022/02/14/putin-macron-talks-highlight-gap-in-russia-expertise-a76375

item.title: Mass protests: lessons from a decade
item.description:
This essay reevaluates the 2011 Bolotnaya protests not as a tactical failure, but as a structural inevitability. Framing the defeat as a Hegelian catalyst, it argues the subsequent crackdown inadvertently served as a "civil society university," transforming a naive public into a deeply politicized society primed for an unpredictable historical synthesis.
The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/12/10/russias-mass-protests-10-years-on-finding-hope-in-apparent-defeat-a75781

item.title: Commentary on pressure against independent media institutions
item.description:
This card summarizes "Commentary on pressure against independent media institutions" published by The Moscow Times on 2021-08-24.
This piece places media freedom in a concrete editorial moment in 2021.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/08/24/the-kremlin-is-coming-for-media-one-by-one-and-society-is-helpless-to-stop-it-a74879

item.title: The Magic Mirror of Russian Public Opinion
item.description:
How public-opinion narratives are shaped by institutions, incentives, and media distribution logic. This card summarizes "The Magic Mirror of Russian Public Opinion" published by The Moscow Times on 2021-07-12.
It connects public opinion with the original text published by The Moscow Times.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/07/12/the-magic-mirror-of-public-opinion-a74492

item.title: The Dilemma of the Great Terror: How to Report Roman Protasevich's Confession?
item.description:
A short summary of media ethics, with a direct link to the original publication.
The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/06/07/the-dilemma-of-the-great-terror-how-to-report-roman-protasevichs-confession-a74125

item.title: Let's Wait Until After the Elections
item.description:
How election-timing rhetoric can postpone decisions and normalize strategic waiting. This card summarizes "Let's Wait Until After the Elections" published by The Moscow Times on 2021-05-17.
It explains electoral timing and links directly to the primary source.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/05/17/lets-wait-until-after-the-elections-a73915

item.title: Dekoder zu Sputnik V, EU-Zulassung und russischer Impfkommunikation
item.description:
Der Dekoder-Artikel behandelt die Debatte um Sputnik V in der EU und zitiert Klishin zur Kommunikationslage in Russland. Er verknuepft die internationale Impfstoffwerbung mit innenpolitischem Misstrauen nach Jahren verschworerischer Staatsrhetorik.
Referenzeintrag dazu, wie deutschsprachige Berichterstattung Klishins Einordnung in der Sputnik-V-Debatte nutzt.
Original source: https://www.dekoder.org/de/article/sputnik-v-impfstoff-zulassung-russland-eu

item.title: Dekoder on Sputnik V approval and Russian vaccine messaging
item.description:
This Dekoder piece discusses the Sputnik V approval debate in the EU and cites commentary from Klishin on communication effects in Russia. The text links external promotion of the vaccine to domestic distrust shaped by years of conspiracy-focused state broadcasting.
Reference card on how German-language coverage used Klishin commentary in the Sputnik V and trust debate.
Original source: https://www.dekoder.org/de/article/sputnik-v-impfstoff-zulassung-russland-eu

item.title: Dekoder sobre Sputnik V, la UE y la narrativa rusa de vacunacion
item.description:
El articulo vincula la promocion externa de la vacuna con la desconfianza interna acumulada por anos de retorica conspirativa estatal.
Referencia sobre como un medio germanoparlante utilizo comentarios de Klishin en el debate de Sputnik V.
Original source: https://www.dekoder.org/de/article/sputnik-v-impfstoff-zulassung-russland-eu

item.title: Dekoder sur Sputnik V, l UE et la communication vaccinale russe
item.description:
Il relie la promotion externe du vaccin a une mefiance interne produite par des annees de rhetorique conspirationniste.
Reference utile sur l usage des analyses de Klishin dans un media germanophone au sujet de Sputnik V.
Original source: https://www.dekoder.org/de/article/sputnik-v-impfstoff-zulassung-russland-eu

item.title: Luiza Rozova and the Helpless Intellectual
item.description:
The piece focuses on elite discourse and explains why the issue mattered in public debate in 2021.
This piece places elite discourse in a concrete editorial moment in 2021.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/03/19/luiza-rozova-and-the-helpless-intellectual-a73301

item.title: Bloomberg zitiert Klishin zur Twitter-Drosselung in Russland
item.description:
Der Bloomberg-Bericht behandelt Russlands Entscheidung, Twitter zu verlangsamen, als Teil eines breiteren Vorgehens gegen westliche Plattformen. Klishin ist nicht Autor, wird aber zitiert, um den Druckaufbau ohne sofortige Vollsperre zu erklaeren.
Externe Referenz, die Klishins Einordnung in der internationalen Berichterstattung zur Plattformpolitik in Russland dokumentiert.
Original source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2021-03-10/russia-takes-aim-at-twitter-over-content-in-unprecedented-crackdown

item.title: Bloomberg quotes Klishin on Russia’s Twitter crackdown
item.description:
This Bloomberg report covers Russia’s move to slow down Twitter as part of a broader crackdown on Western social platforms. Klishin is not the author, but he is quoted in the piece as it explains how the authorities were tightening pressure while still stopping short of a full block.
External feature card documenting how Bloomberg used Klishin commentary while reporting Russia’s platform pressure strategy.
Original source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2021-03-10/russia-takes-aim-at-twitter-over-content-in-unprecedented-crackdown

item.title: Bloomberg cita a Klishin sobre la desaceleracion de Twitter en Rusia
item.description:
Este reporte de Bloomberg cubre la decision rusa de ralentizar Twitter dentro de una presion mas amplia sobre plataformas occidentales. Klishin no es autor del texto, pero aparece citado para explicar la escalada sin llegar al bloqueo total.
Referencia externa que muestra el uso de comentarios de Klishin en una cobertura internacional sobre politica digital rusa.
Original source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2021-03-10/russia-takes-aim-at-twitter-over-content-in-unprecedented-crackdown

item.title: Bloomberg cite Klishin sur le ralentissement de Twitter en Russie
item.description:
Ce reportage de Bloomberg decrit la decision russe de ralentir Twitter dans un cycle plus large de pression sur les plateformes occidentales. Klishin n en est pas l auteur, mais il est cite dans l article pour expliquer la logique d escalade sans blocage total.
Reference externe qui atteste l usage de commentaires de Klishin dans une couverture economique internationale sur la regulation des plateformes.
Original source: https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2021-03-10/russia-takes-aim-at-twitter-over-content-in-unprecedented-crackdown

item.title: Four Ways the Kremlin Could Block Western Social Networks in Russia
item.description:
Which technical and political mechanisms can be used to restrict social-network access. This card summarizes "Four Ways the Kremlin Could Block Western Social Networks in Russia" published by The Moscow Times on 2021-02-12.
It connects social network regulation with the original text published by The Moscow Times.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/02/12/four-ways-the-kremlin-could-block-western-social-networks-in-russia-a72920

item.title: The Capitol Storming Gives Russians an Escape From Their Reality
item.description:
This The Moscow Times (2021) publication examines comparative media framing and summarizes the main argument.
The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/01/14/the-capitol-storming-gives-russians-an-escape-from-their-reality-a72598

item.title: Warning! An Army of Russian Trolls Has Invaded TikTok
item.description:
How platform dynamics and troll methods move from domestic messaging into wider information spaces. This card summarizes "Warning!
It explains disinformation and platforms and links directly to the primary source.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/12/18/warning-an-army-of-russian-trolls-has-invaded-tiktok-a72410

item.title: Why Russians Dislike Good Russians in American Films No Less Than Bad Russians
item.description:
The article outlines cultural representation in clear terms and places it in editorial context.
It connects cultural representation with the original text published by The Moscow Times.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/11/20/why-russians-dislike-good-russians-in-american-films-no-less-than-bad-russians-a72097

item.title: Great Russian Literature Versus One Tweet by Stephen King
item.description:
How cultural symbols and representation frames affect trust, identity, and audience reaction. This card summarizes "Great Russian Literature Versus One Tweet by Stephen King" published by The Moscow Times on 2020-11-09.
The summary gives a clear reference point for later comparisons.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/11/09/great-russian-literature-versus-one-tweet-by-stephen-king-a71993

item.title: Anonymous Telegram Channels Are Shaping Russia's Daily Life
item.description:
The piece focuses on platform influence and explains why the issue mattered in public debate in 2020.
It explains platform influence and links directly to the primary source.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/09/28/anonymous-telegram-channels-are-shaping-russias-reality-a71588

item.title: Why Do Russians Resent Western Media Over The
item.description:
Why protest outcomes that look like defeat can still produce long-term civic learning. This card summarizes "Belarus Coverage Framing: The Moscow Times (2020-08-14) - Why Do Russians Resent Western Media Over The" published by The Moscow Times on 2020-08-14.
This piece places belarus coverage framing in a concrete editorial moment in 2020.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/08/14/why-do-russians-resent-western-media-over-the-protests-in-belarus-a71155

item.title: Coronavirus Spoils Putin's 'Forever-in-Power' Public Vote
item.description:
The column explains how the pandemic disrupted a carefully staged constitutional plebiscite. Klishin argues that the Kremlin lost timing control and faced a strategic bind where postponement and continuation both carried rising political costs.
Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/04/16/coronavirus-spoils-putins-forever-in-power-vote-a70014

item.title: Why Medvedev's resignation and Putin's amendments changed nothing
item.description:
This Russian-language Moscow Times column argues that both events were staged inside a fixed power design. Klishin presents personnel rotation and constitutional theater as symbolic moves that changed optics but did not alter the real center of decision-making.
Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/01/17/10-2-a68947

item.title: Who owns Russian? And who owns English?
item.description:
The column examines how language is politicized as an instrument of cultural control. Klishin compares claims over Russian and English to show that language policy often masks power struggles over identity, legitimacy, and who may define public norms.
Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2019/11/11/9-a68123

item.title: Why shoot Navalny if you can bankrupt him
item.description:
This Russian-language column frames financial pressure as a central tool of modern repression. Instead of visible violence, authorities can exhaust opponents through fines, frozen accounts, and legal costs while preserving the appearance of procedural legality.
Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2019/10/30/klishin-why-hold-mass-executions-when-bankruptcies-work-a67972

item.title: How Putin Is Bankrupting the Russian Opposition
item.description:
This English column describes bankruptcy tactics as a calibrated instrument of repression. Instead of spectacular crackdowns, the state can deplete opposition capacity through legal debts, penalties, and procedural pressure while preserving formal plausibility.
Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2019/10/30/how-putin-is-bankrupting-the-russian-opposition-a67971

item.title: How the death penalty debate linked to a ban on negative Ukraine coverage
item.description:
Klishin connects two media agendas to show how distraction campaigns are built. The text argues that emotionally loaded topics can be amplified to redirect public attention and normalize selective information boundaries around politically sensitive narratives.
Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2019/10/16/8-2-a67761

item.title: Russias New Heroes Are Those Who Simply Say Things as They Are
item.description:
Klishin analyzes why plain factual speech became politically meaningful in a distorted information environment. The piece argues that when institutions fail, even minimal honesty from insiders can shift public attention and recalibrate legitimacy.
Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2019/09/26/russias-new-heroes-are-those-who-simply-say-things-as-they-are-a67400

item.title: Why Russian Journalists Find Refuge in Propaganda Outlets
item.description:
The article examines structural reasons why independent journalists move into propaganda institutions. Klishin argues that contraction of the independent market and career precarity create conditions where ideological compromise becomes a professional survival strategy.
Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2019/04/05/why-russian-journalists-find-refuge-in-propaganda-outlets-a65108

item.title: The music of the media desert
item.description:
This Vedomosti column describes how shrinking pluralism produces a repetitive and hostile media environment. Klishin argues that the system rewards noise and intimidation, while public discussion loses nuance, memory, and professional standards.
Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.
Original source: https://www.vedomosti.ru/opinion/columns/2018/12/06/788550-muzika-mediinoi-pustini

item.title: Moscow: clean city, cleansed city
item.description:
The column uses urban cleanliness as a political metaphor for controlled public space. Klishin contrasts aesthetic order with social exclusion and argues that visible improvement can coexist with narrowed civic participation and managed dissent.
Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.
Original source: https://www.vedomosti.ru/opinion/columns/2018/05/24/770522-moskva-chistaya-i-zachischennaya

item.title: From Kremlin Web Brigades to Russian Troll Factories
item.description:
The VSquare article tracks the evolution from early online brigades to industrialized troll operations and cites Klishin in that discussion. This card is included as an external analytical reference where his assessments are reused in broader reporting.
Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.
Original source: https://vsquare.org/from-kremlin-web-brigades-to-russian-troll-factories/

item.title: Freedom on the Net 2016 erfasst Druckmuster im Umfeld von Klishin und Kollegen
item.description:
Das Russland-Kapitel von Freedom House 2016 dokumentiert digitalen Druck und Angriffe auf Konten unabhaengiger Akteure.
Institutionelle Quelle zur Einordnung digitaler Einschuechterung gegen unabhaengige Medien- und Zivilakteure.
Original source: https://freedomhouse.org/country/russia/freedom-net/2016

item.title: Freedom on the Net 2016 records pressure linked to Klishin and peers
item.description:
Freedom House's 2016 Russia chapter documents cyber pressure and account targeting against independent actors and investigators. The report is included as an institutional record in the wider timeline of digital intimidation and online-control practices.
Institutional source supporting chronology and context for digital intimidation patterns around independent media and activists.
Original source: https://freedomhouse.org/country/russia/freedom-net/2016

item.title: Freedom on the Net 2016 registra presion digital vinculada a Klishin y su entorno
item.description:
El capitulo sobre Rusia de Freedom House en 2016 documenta presion digital y ataques contra cuentas de actores independientes. Esta entrada se incluye como registro institucional dentro de la cronologia de intimidacion y control de internet.
Fuente institucional para contextualizar patrones de intimidacion digital contra actores civicos y periodisticos.
Original source: https://freedomhouse.org/country/russia/freedom-net/2016

item.title: Freedom on the Net 2016 documente des pressions liees a Klishin et ses pairs
item.description:
Le chapitre Russie de Freedom House en 2016 documente des pressions numeriques et des attaques contre des comptes d acteurs independants. Cette entree conserve une source institutionnelle dans la chronologie des pratiques d intimidation et de controle en ligne.
Source institutionnelle utile pour documenter le contexte des intimidations numeriques visant acteurs civiques et medatiques.
Original source: https://freedomhouse.org/country/russia/freedom-net/2016

item.title: Private Authoritarianism: How Facebook and Google Threaten Democracy
item.description:
This Carnegie essay argues that major private platforms gained political power without democratic accountability. It examines how algorithmic ranking, moderation policies, and opaque product decisions can shape public debate as strongly as state media controls. The text is included as an early framing of platform power as a governance problem rather than only a technical or market issue.
Strengthens the archive with an early argument about platform governance, accountability, and democratic risk beyond state media frameworks.
Original source: https://carnegieendowment.org/ru/russia-eurasia/posts/2016/09/chastnyj-avtoritarizm-kak-facebook-i-google-ugrozhayut-demokratii

item.title: Web of Control: The Future of the Russian Internet
item.description:
This Carnegie analysis outlines scenarios for state control over the Russian internet. It tracks how policy, platform pressure, and institutional coordinators transformed once-unthinkable restrictions into routine options in public governance.
Entry added to include this publication in the English corpus with a verified source link.
Original source: https://carnegieendowment.org/posts/2016/01/web-of-control-the-future-of-the-russian-internet

item.title: Gazeta.ru-Interview zur moeglichen Sperre von YouTube und Google
item.description:
Dieses Gazeta. ru-Interview dokumentiert einen Expertenkommentar zu technischen und politischen Szenarien einer Plattformsperre in Russland. Klishin wird zu moeglichen Folgen einer Einschraenkung von YouTube oder Google sowie zur breiteren Logik digitaler Kontrolle zitiert.
Referenzeintrag zur fruehen Berichterstattung ueber Plattformsperren und digitale Kontrolle in Russland.
Original source: https://www.gazeta.ru/tech/news/2016/01/16/n_8127887.shtml

item.title: Gazeta.ru interview on possible blocking of YouTube and Google
item.description:
This Gazeta. ru interview records an expert comment on technical and political scenarios of blocking major platforms in Russia. Klishin is cited on the likely consequences of restricting YouTube or Google and the broader logic of digital control.
Reference entry for early media discussion of platform blocking risks in Russia.
Original source: https://www.gazeta.ru/tech/news/2016/01/16/n_8127887.shtml

item.title: N 8127887.shtml
item.description:
Esta entrevista de Gazeta. ru recoge un comentario experto sobre escenarios tecnicos y politicos de bloqueo de grandes plataformas en Rusia. Klishin aparece citado sobre las consecuencias probables de restringir YouTube o Google y la logica mas amplia de control digital.
Entrada de referencia sobre debate temprano en medios acerca de bloqueo de plataformas en Rusia.
Original source: https://www.gazeta.ru/tech/news/2016/01/16/n_8127887.shtml

item.title: Entretien Gazeta.ru sur un possible blocage de YouTube et Google
item.description:
Cet entretien de Gazeta. ru conserve un commentaire expert sur les scenarios techniques et politiques de blocage des grandes plateformes en Russie. Klishin y est cite a propos des consequences probables d une restriction de YouTube ou Google et de la logique plus large de controle numerique.
Entree de reference sur les discussions mediales precoces autour des risques de blocage de plateformes en Russie.
Original source: https://www.gazeta.ru/tech/news/2016/01/16/n_8127887.shtml

item.title: JSIS-Profil: digitaler Patriotismus und nationalistische Agenda in russischen Medien
item.description:
Dieses JSIS-Profil untersucht digitalen Patriotismus und nationalistisches Narrativmanagement in der russischen Medienpolitik. Es dient als institutionelle Kontextquelle zu Rechtsunschaerfe, Zensurpraktiken und Bezuengen zu Klishins Medienanalyse.
Referenzeintrag zur Verknuepfung akademischer Politikforschung und Klishin-bezogenem Medienkontext.
Original source: https://jsis.washington.edu/news/russia-media-profile-digital-patriotism-nationalist-agenda/

item.title: JSIS profile: digital patriotism and nationalist agenda in Russian media
item.description:
This JSIS profile examines digital patriotism and nationalist narrative management in Russian media policy. It is included as an institutional context source that references legal ambiguity, censorship practices, and commentary associated with Klishin's media analysis work.
Reference entry linking academic policy analysis of Russian media controls to Klishin-related analytical context.
Original source: https://jsis.washington.edu/news/russia-media-profile-digital-patriotism-nationalist-agenda/

item.title: Perfil JSIS: patriotismo digital y agenda nacionalista en medios rusos
item.description:
Este perfil de JSIS estudia el patriotismo digital y la gestion de narrativas nacionalistas en la politica mediatica rusa. Se incluye como fuente institucional sobre ambiguedad legal, practicas de censura y contexto analitico asociado a Klishin.
Entrada de referencia que conecta analisis academico de control mediatico ruso con contexto analitico ligado a Klishin.
Original source: https://jsis.washington.edu/news/russia-media-profile-digital-patriotism-nationalist-agenda/

item.title: Profil JSIS : patriotisme numerique et agenda nationaliste dans les medias russes
item.description:
Ce profil JSIS analyse le patriotisme numerique et la gestion des recits nationalistes dans la politique mediatique russe. Il est conserve comme source institutionnelle de contexte sur l ambiguite juridique, les pratiques de censure et les analyses associees a Klishin.
Entree de reference reliant analyse academique des controles mediatique russes et contexte analytique lie a Klishin.
Original source: https://jsis.washington.edu/news/russia-media-profile-digital-patriotism-nationalist-agenda/

item.title: Do Revolutions Grow Out of Social Media?
item.description:
This Carnegie essay challenges the cliche that social networks directly produce revolutions. It argues that platforms can accelerate visibility, coordination, and emotional contagion, but they do not replace institutions, organization, and political opportunity structures. Using post-Soviet examples, the text separates communication effects from causal claims about regime change and warns against technological determinism in media analysis.
Useful for separating media amplification from political causality in debates about digital protest dynamics across post-Soviet contexts.
Original source: https://carnegieendowment.org/ru/posts/2015/10/samizdat-i-zameshenie-rastut-li-iz-socsetej-revolyucii

item.title: Bot Army: Why the Kremlin Fails at Soft Power
item.description:
This column deconstructs the Kremlin bot infrastructure, framing it not as potent soft power, but as a reactive domestic tactic. Operating from a semantic void, these networks broadcast informational noise that merely exploits Western systemic anxieties rather than projecting genuine ideological influence.
Useful reference card that situates Klishin's work through a dated and verifiable Carnegie.
Original source: https://carnegie.ru/commentary/60728

item.title: What Sparked Moldova's Second Maidan?
item.description:
This Carnegie analysis examines renewed protest mobilization in Moldova and asks why frustration returned after earlier electoral breakthroughs. The text tracks the gap between reform promises and lived outcomes, showing how repeated cycles of disappointment can reactivate street politics even when formal institutions remain unchanged. It is included as a regional case study of post-Soviet protest recurrence and narrative fatigue.
Adds a comparative regional case on protest recurrence, useful for interpreting delayed political effects after apparent electoral wins.
Original source: https://carnegieendowment.org/ru/posts/2015/05/protiv-chego-povtorno-sobiralsya-moldavskij-majdan
